query_string: The Man Who Would Be King

tossups.question: Packet 3 1. The second item in a contract that appears in this story stipulates that two men must abstain from looking at any type of liquor and any race of women. A character in this story asks the narrator to deliver the cryptic message “He has gone south for the week” to a man in a train station. It includes a scene in which a man is bitten while trying to kiss his prospective bride, and, because he (*) bleeds, the people become convinced that he is a man instead of a god. In this story, Peachey Carnahan survives being crucified onto two pine trees, though he dies of exposure after being kicked out of Kafiristan. Its narrator is an official in British India who is related the tale of Daniel Dravot, a character inspired by the empire-building exploits of James Brooke. For 10 points, name this story by Rudyard Kipling.
tossups.question_sanitized: Packet 3 1. The second item in a contract that appears in this story stipulates that two men must abstain from looking at any type of liquor and any race of women. In this story, Peachey Carnahan survives being crucified onto two pine trees, though he dies of exposure after being kicked out of Kafiristan. Its narrator is an official in British India who is related the tale of Daniel Dravot, a character inspired by the empire-building exploits of James Brooke. For 10 points, name this story by Rudyard Kipling.

tossups.question: The narrator of this work relays the message “He has gone South for the week” to the red man at Marwar Junction, and works as a correspondent for the Backwoodsman. One character in this short story is executed by standing on a rope bridge while it is cut down, and another man is recognized after surviving (+) crucifixion by his inch-thick unibrow. Two characters start a lodge of the Freemasons in the Temple of Imbra after gaining a following defending an outnumbered group of archers with their (*) Martini-Henry rifles. Despite the warnings of Billy Fish, one man insists on forcibly taking an Afghan bride, and he loses power when she responds by biting his nose. For ten points, name this short story in which Daniel Dravot and Peachey Carnehan decide to be rulers of Kafiristan written by Rudyard Kipling.
tossups.answer: The Man Who Would Be King
tossups.answer_sanitized: The Man Who Would Be King

bonuses.leadin: Answer the following about references to Alexander the Great in world literature for 10 points each.
parts: This Malian epic refers to Alexander as "the mighty king of gold and silver" and the focal point of a history of kings that the griot Balla Fasseke tells to its protagonist.
In this story, Daniel Dravot is revered as a second coming of Alexander the Great by a tribe descended from his army in the Pakistani region of Kafiristan.
In this epic, a talking tree that Alexander finds at the end of the world tells him that he will die in Babylon. In this epic poem, Rostam accidentally kills his son, Sohrab, in battle.
answers: "The Man who Would Be King" [by Rudyard Kipling]
answers_sanitized: "The Man who Would Be King" [by Rudyard Kipling]

In this story, Daniel Dravot is revered as a second coming of Alexander the Great by a tribe descended from his army in the Pakistani region of Kafiristan.
In this epic, a talking tree that Alexander finds at the end of the world tells him that he will die in Babylon. In this epic poem, Rostam accidentally kills his son, Sohrab, in battle.

bonuses.leadin: Two soldiers in this story pass through the Khyber Pass with a caravan while disguised as toy salesmen. For 10 points each:
parts: Name this novella in which the wife of Daniel Dravot bites him, revealing to Kafiristan through the resulting trickle of blood that he is not a god.

This author of Gunga Din, who showed a reversal of colonial power structures when a white man falls in a ravine full of cholera victims in "The Strange Ride of Morrowbie Jukes," wrote "The Man Who Would Be King".

Kipling was among the first authors to make a great deal of money selling his work in these places, where the narrator of "The Man Who Would Be King" delivers the message "He has gone South for the week."
answers: train stations [or railway stations; or railway junctions; or other reasonable equivalents]
answers_sanitized: The Man Who Would Be King
train stations [or railway stations; or railway junctions; or other reasonable equivalents]

bonuses.leadin: The central characters of this story luck into success when it turns out the priests of Kafiristan are aware of Masonic rituals, but their schemes run into trouble when Daniel Dravot is bitten by a girl, revealing their mortality. For 10 points each:
parts: Name this story, told as a flashback by Peachy Carnahan, in which Dravot and Carnahan attempt to follow in the footsteps of Alexander the Great.
Teshoo Lama searches for the "River of the Arrow" in this novel by the same author, whose title character works with Mahbub Ali and Hurree Babu to spy for the British in the Great Game.
Kim and The Man Who Would Be King were both written by this Indian-born British author of the Just-So Stories and The Jungle Book.

bonuses.leadin: One character in this story is revealed not to be a god when a bite draws his blood. For 10 points each:
parts: Identify this short story, in which Daniel Dravot and Peachy Carnehan become rulers of a short-lived empire in Kafiristan thanks to their guns and knowledge of Freemasonry.
This author wrote “The Man Who Would Be King.” Other stories of his like “Mowgli’s Brothers” and “Toomai of the Elephants” were included in his The Jungle Book.
This Kipling children’s collection includes “How the Camel Got His Hump” and “How the Leopard Got His Spots.” They fancifully explain where certain animals’ distinguishing features come from.
answers_sanitized: Just So Stories for Little Children
